channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Tobacco Farming in the New Nicotine Era: Why Indian Farmers Struggle to Transition — Contributed by Samrat Chowdhery
item.description: The transition to a new nicotine era is raising complex questions for tobacco-growing communities, particularly in countries where farming livelihoods remain closely tied to combustible tobacco markets. Indian farmers face structural barriers to diversification, including limited access to alternative crops, market uncertainty, debt pressures and dependence on established tobacco supply chains. The debate highlights that tobacco harm reduction and tobacco control policy cannot focus only on consumers, but must also consider the economic realities of producers. A sustainable shift away from smoking-related harm will require evidence-based regulation, viable agricultural transition pathways and policies that support farmers rather than leaving them behind. (Published on May 29, 2026by2firsts.com)

item.title: Reach Out, Don't Shun: What World No Tobacco Day Used to Mean
item.description: World No Tobacco Day is renewing debate over whether global tobacco control should focus more on engagement with smokers rather than exclusion or stigma. Harm reduction advocates argue that people who smoke are more likely to benefit from accurate information, practical support and access to lower-risk alternatives than from shame-based messaging. The discussion highlights concerns that abstinence-only approaches may overlook the behavioural realities of nicotine dependence and the needs of smokers who have not quit through conventional methods. A more inclusive public health strategy would prioritise empathy, risk reduction and evidence-based pathways away from combustible tobacco. (Published on May 29, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Illinois’ Latest Flavor Ban Proposal Ignores Falling Youth Vaping and Smoking Rates
item.description: Illinois’ latest flavour ban proposal is adding to debate over whether nicotine policy is keeping pace with current youth use trends. Critics argue that falling rates of both youth vaping and smoking should prompt more careful assessment of whether broad flavour restrictions are proportionate or evidence-based. Harm reduction advocates warn that limiting flavoured vaping products may reduce the appeal of lower-risk alternatives for adult smokers while doing little to address illicit supply. The debate reflects wider tensions between youth prevention, adult smoking cessation, consumer behaviour and the need for regulation grounded in reliable population-level data. (Published on May 29, 2026bythr101.org)

item.title: Brussels promised to listen to consumers. Now it must prove it
item.description: The EU’s review of tobacco and nicotine regulation is placing renewed attention on whether consumer perspectives are being meaningfully included in policymaking. Harm reduction advocates argue that people who use safer nicotine products have direct experience of switching away from combustible tobacco and should not be excluded from decisions that affect their choices. The debate highlights concerns that regulation shaped without consumer input may overlook real-world behaviour, risk perception and the practical barriers smokers face when trying to quit. A more evidence-based policy process would recognise lived experience as a necessary part of understanding how nicotine regulation affects public health outcomes. (Published on May 29, 2026bytheparliamentmagazine.eu)

item.title: Sweden set to block EU tobacco tax rules reform
item.description: Sweden’s opposition to the EU tobacco tax reform is adding further pressure to an already contested debate over nicotine taxation and national policy autonomy. The proposal would raise minimum excise duties on cigarettes while also introducing EU-level minimum taxes for newer products, including e-cigarettes and nicotine pouches. Critics argue that applying steep or poorly differentiated taxes to lower-risk nicotine products could weaken harm reduction incentives and reduce the price advantage that helps smokers move away from combustible tobacco. The dispute highlights wider tensions between harmonised EU fiscal policy, Member State sovereignty, illicit trade concerns and evidence-based regulation built around relative risk. (Published on May 29, 2026byeuractiv.com)
